sections.plain_summary: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI) means your pancreas doesn't make enough digestive enzymes to break down food properly. This leads to poor nutrient absorption, weight loss, and fatty, loose stools. It's treatable with pancreatic enzyme replacement and dietary changes.
sections.detailed_summary: The pancreas produces enzymes that digest fats, proteins, and carbohydrates in the small intestine. In EPI, enzyme production falls below 10% of normal capacity, preventing adequate food breakdown. This causes malabsorption—nutrients pass through the digestive tract unabsorbed. Common causes include chronic pancreatitis (most frequent), cystic fibrosis, pancreatic cancer, diabetes, and post-surgery changes. Symptoms typically appear when enzyme output drops by 90% or more. EPI is manageable but requires lifelong enzyme supplementation and dietary adaptation. Early diagnosis and treatment prevent malnutrition and improve quality of life.

prevalence.summary: EPI affects roughly 1–3% of the general population, but is much more common in specific groups.
prevalence.estimate: 1–3% overall; 30–40% in chronic pancreatitis; 85–90% in cystic fibrosis
prevalence.notes: Prevalence varies widely by underlying cause. Chronic pancreatitis is the most common cause in developed countries. Cystic fibrosis patients have the highest rates. Many cases go undiagnosed because symptoms overlap with other digestive conditions. Incidence increases with age and alcohol use.

subgroups.name: Chronic pancreatitis–related EPI
subgroups.description: Results from long-standing inflammation and scarring of pancreatic tissue, most often due to alcohol abuse, idiopathic causes, or autoimmune pancreatitis. Progressive loss of enzyme-producing cells.
distinguishing_features: History of recurrent or chronic abdominal pain
May have elevated blood glucose and fecal chymotrypsin deficiency
Imaging may show pancreatic atrophy or calcification

subgroups.name: Cystic fibrosis–related EPI
subgroups.description: Genetic disorder causing thick, sticky secretions that block pancreatic ducts. EPI is present in 85–90% of CF patients and usually appears in infancy or early childhood.
distinguishing_features: Elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) on newborn screening
Concurrent lung disease and salty sweat

subgroups.name: Post-surgical EPI
subgroups.description: Develops after pancreatic resection, gastric bypass, or other abdominal surgery that disrupts normal anatomy or enzyme delivery.
distinguishing_features: May develop months or years post-op
Symptoms improve with enzyme dosing and dietary modification

subgroups.name: Idiopathic EPI
subgroups.description: No identifiable cause (no pancreatitis history, normal imaging, no cystic fibrosis). May represent early or mild pancreatic dysfunction.
distinguishing_features: Reduced fecal elastase without obvious etiology
Less severe enzyme deficiency than chronic pancreatitis

symptoms.notes: Oily appearance, floats, difficult to flush. Hallmark sign of fat malabsorption. May be worse after fatty meals.

symptoms.symptom: Chronic diarrhea or loose bowel movements
symptoms.notes: Often watery or mucoid, 3–4+ times daily. Can be intermittent or constant.
Results from undigested food fermenting in the colon. Worse after meals.
Variable intensity and location; may or may not be present depending on underlying cause. More prominent in chronic pancreatitis.
Due to calorie and nutrient malabsorption despite adequate or increased food intake. Can be gradual.
From protein malabsorption and calorie deficit. May worsen over time without treatment.
Fat-soluble vitamins poorly absorbed. Can cause bone loss (D, K), night blindness (A), and bleeding tendency (K).
From calorie and nutrient malabsorption, anemia, or deficiency states.

symptoms.symptom: Food intolerance or increased symptoms after eating
symptoms.notes: Especially after fatty or greasy foods. High-fat meals worsen steatorrhea.

key_patterns: Chronic loose or fatty stools plus unintentional weight loss despite eating normally
Abdominal bloating, gas, and cramping that worsen after meals
Symptoms that improve with low-fat diet or pancreatic enzyme supplements
History of chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, or pancreatic surgery
Cluster of digestive symptoms lasting more than 4 weeks

quick_checks.check: Describe your recent stools: are they pale, oily, difficult to flush, or unusually loose?
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Fatty or loose stools (steatorrhea) are a hallmark of EPI. Normal stools should brown and formed. Floating, greasy stools suggest fat malabsorption.

quick_checks.check: Over the past 3 months, have you lost weight without intentionally dieting?
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Unintentional weight loss is common in moderate-to-severe EPI due to calorie and nutrient loss in stools.

quick_checks.check: Do your symptoms improve when you eat a low-fat meal or take over-the-counter digestive enzymes?
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Temporary improvement with low-fat eating or enzyme supplements suggests malabsorption, but is not diagnostic.

quick_checks.check: Do you have a history of chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, or abdominal surgery?
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: These are major risk factors for EPI. History increases likelihood significantly.

quick_checks.check: Have you noticed bloating, gas, or cramping that worsens after eating?
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Postprandial (after-meal) bloating and cramping suggest undigested food fermenting in the colon, consistent with EPI.

sections.relevance_signal: **Fatty, pale, foul-smelling stools** that float. Unintentional weight loss. Bloating and discomfort after fatty meals. Worse with a history of pancreatitis or heavy alcohol use.
